[[On the Dial]] DOMY carries the Third Quarter to the LEFT of the moon ring, opposite the First Quarter's right, its marker disc half lit and emptying, the silver glow on the principal instant. The Moon is halfway home, from the full bottom back toward the dark top.

Waning Crescent.title: Waning Crescent
Waning Crescent.base:
[[The Sky]] The last of the light shrinks to a thin bow on the Moon's eastern edge, the mirror of the young crescent that opened the cycle. It rises before dawn and sets in mid-morning, so it hangs low in the east in the hour before sunrise — the old Moon of the morning, earthshine often filling the darkened disc — before it slides back into the Sun's glare and the round begins once more.

[[The Story]] Astrology names this the balsamic Moon, the phase of rest, surrender and the quiet before renewal. It belongs to Hecate again and to the old wise woman — the waning light read as age and its wisdom, the ground lying fallow, the breath drawn before the next beginning. It is the emptying that makes the New Moon's filling possible: the cycle's own Sabbath.

[[On the Dial]] On the moon ring the marker closes on the TOP again, its disc a thinning crescent nearly spent — clockwise past the Third Quarter and almost back to the New Moon at 12h, where the whole round will start over.

Summer_Solstice.title: Summer Solstice
Summer_Solstice.base:
[[The Sky]] The summer solstice is the instant the sun's apparent path along the ecliptic reaches its northernmost point, ecliptic longitude 90 degrees, and its declination peaks at +23.44 degrees — Earth's own axial tilt, no more and no less. At that moment the sun stands directly overhead at local noon for every observer on the Tropic of Cancer, and everywhere north of the equator the day is as long, and the night as short, as the year allows.

[[On the Dial]] The year wheel places this instant exactly at the top — not approximated to the nearest day but read off the bundled ephemeris and pinned to the true crossing, the same standard the dial holds its solar-noon hexagram to every single day. On Sundays the Seasons pointer lights this arm together with its opposite number at the bottom, the year's two solstices glowing at once — the double center's own year-long echo, the Ruler's noon and the Servant's midnight meeting a full revolution apart. The badge borrows the shape of that daily hexagram for the year's own noon: a golden six-rayed star crossing behind a sun pinned motionless at its highest point, a gnomon below it casting a shadow reduced, at true solar noon on the Tropic itself, to almost nothing at all.

Winter_Solstice.title: Winter Solstice
Winter_Solstice.base:
[[The Sky]] The winter solstice mirrors its summer twin exactly: the sun's ecliptic longitude reaches 270 degrees, its declination bottoms out at -23.44 degrees, and it stands directly overhead at local noon only for observers on the Tropic of Capricorn. North of the equator this is the shortest day and longest night the year offers — at high enough latitude, as at Tromso, a noon the sun barely bothers to clear.

[[On the Dial]] The wheel plants this instant at the bottom, exactly opposite its summer counterpart and held to the same ephemeris-true standard rather than a rounded date. It shares the Sunday privilege too: when the Seasons pointer lights both solstice arms together, this is the dark half of that pair, the Servant's own hour stretched from a nightly eclipse to a yearly one. The badge answers gold with silver: a sun barely clearing a snow-bound horizon at its lowest arc of the year, a pale six-rayed hexagram behind it, and a gnomon below casting the longest shadow the year ever asks of it.

Equinox.title: Equinox
Equinox.base:
[[The Sky]] Twice a year the sun's path along the ecliptic crosses the celestial equator and its declination touches exactly zero — around March and September, the two moments this one badge stands for equally, since 'the day of perfect balance' reads the same whichever crossing brought it. Day and night are only approximately equal on the date itself; the sun's disc and the air's own bending of light lift sunrise early and hold sunset late by a few minutes each, so the truly equal split, the equilux, falls a few days to one side — a discrepancy small enough to ignore on the wrist and large enough that this dial's own standards insist on naming it anyway.

[[On the Dial]] The wheel plants the two equinoxes at left and right, at the 06h and 18h clock positions — the very slots the octa pointer already gives to dawn and dusk on the day below — each held to its true crossing rather than a calendar convenience. Where Spring wears Goethe's green union of yellow and blue, the Turning Points badge renders the same balance in metal rather than pigment: the badge splits exactly in half, polished gold meeting oxidized silver at two opposite seams, a gnomon standing dead center and casting a shadow precisely as long as it is tall, one half of the disc lit in full daylight and the other in full silver-blue night — noon's metal and midnight's metal, in perfect equilibrium, for the one day a year they actually are.

Solar_Overview.title: Solar Eclipses
Solar_Overview.base:
[[The Sky]] A solar eclipse happens only at New Moon, when the Moon passes directly between the Earth and the Sun and throws its shadow onto the daylit face of the planet. Because the Moon's orbit is tilted about five degrees to Earth's, most New Moons miss — the shadow passes above or below us — so an eclipse falls only when the New Moon lands near a node, the crossing point of the two orbits. Two to five solar eclipses happen somewhere in the world each year, but any one spot waits centuries for a total one, because the dark central shadow, the umbra, is never more than a couple of hundred kilometres wide.

[[The Four Kinds]] What you see depends on how the shadow's tip meets the ground. If the umbra reaches you, the Sun is hidden completely — a TOTAL eclipse. If the Moon is near the far end of its orbit and looks too small to cover the whole disc, a bright ring is left around it — an ANNULAR eclipse, the ring of fire. If only the outer shadow, the penumbra, brushes you, the Sun is merely bitten — a PARTIAL eclipse. And a rare few, HYBRID eclipses, are total along the middle of their track and annular at the ends, where the curve of the Earth lifts the ground just out of the umbra's reach.

[[On the Dial]] DOMY reads its eclipses from the bundled Deep Time catalogue and shows one ONLY within a three-hour window around its true instant. A solar eclipse belongs to the EARTH marker: it relocates to the ring band, swaps its face for the black eclipsed-Sun plate, and lights a glow whose colour and strength name the kind — as the four chapters that follow describe. If the shadow's path runs far from your city, or the Sun has already set, the glow mutes to a quiet silver and the hover tells you why: the eclipse is real, but not yours to see from here.

Solar_Total.title: Total Solar Eclipse
Solar_Total.base:
[[The Sky]] In a total solar eclipse the Moon covers the Sun's disc exactly — a coincidence of scale the sky owes to arithmetic, the Sun about four hundred times wider than the Moon and about four hundred times farther away, so the two appear the same size. For a few minutes the umbra sweeps a narrow path across the Earth at over a thousand kilometres an hour, and inside that path the day goes dark.

[[What You See]] Totality is unlike anything else in the sky. The temperature drops, bright stars come out at noon, and around the black disc the Sun's pearl-white CORONA — its million-degree outer atmosphere, invisible at any other time — stands open to the naked eye. A ruby bead of the chromosphere flashes at the rim as the last light goes and again as it returns. This is the only moment it is ever safe to look at the Sun unfiltered, and only during totality itself.

[[On the Dial]] The Earth marker wears the black eclipsed-Sun plate and its glow burns at FULL strength — the corona drawn as light rather than as a picture, exactly as our canon promised: no new art, the halo does the work. Total is the strongest state the dial knows.

[[How Often]] Somewhere on Earth roughly once every eighteen months; over any single town, on average about once in 375 years.

[[The Anchor]] Herodotus tells of the eclipse of 585 BCE that stopped a battle: the Medes and Lydians, five years at war, saw "the day turned suddenly to night," took it for a sign, and made peace on the spot. Thales of Miletus was said to have foretold it — the first eclipse in recorded history a philosopher claimed to predict.

Solar_Annular.title: Annular Solar Eclipse
Solar_Annular.base:
[[The Sky]] An annular eclipse is a total eclipse that falls short. The alignment is perfect, but the Moon is near apogee, the far point of its slightly oval orbit, where it looks a shade too small to cover the Sun. The umbra's tip stops short of the ground; only its continuation past that point, the antumbra, reaches you.

[[What You See]] The Moon slides dead-centre over the Sun and leaves a brilliant, unbroken RING of sunlight around itself — the ring of fire. The sky dims but never falls to night, no corona appears, and it is NEVER safe to look without a proper filter: a blinding sliver of the Sun's surface is showing the entire time.

[[On the Dial]] Annular shares the black-sun plate with total, but its glow burns in its OWN hotter orange-red — the ring of fire given its own colour, at full strength — so the two states that look most alike in the sky never read the same on the dial.

[[How Often]] Slightly more common than total eclipses: the Moon spends a little more of its orbit far from Earth than near it, so a few fall each decade worldwide.

[[The Anchor]] Early astronomers puzzled long over how the very same geometry could hide the Sun completely one year and leave a bright ring the next. The answer — the Moon's own changing distance along its ellipse — was one of the clean confirmations that the lunar orbit is not a circle but an oval, exactly as Kepler's laws require.

Solar_Partial.title: Partial Solar Eclipse
Solar_Partial.base:
[[The Sky]] Every total and annular eclipse is partial for the wide regions on either side of its central path, and some eclipses are partial for everyone — the umbra misses the Earth entirely, sailing above a pole, and only the penumbra ever touches the surface.

[[What You See]] The Moon takes a curved bite out of the Sun and no more. The light dims a little if the bite is deep, shadows sharpen, and pinholes — the gaps between leaves, the holes in a colander — cast tiny crescent suns across the ground. It is never safe to view unfiltered; no part of the Sun is ever covered enough.

[[On the Dial]] Partial is the one solar state whose glow still scales with MAGNITUDE — how much of the Sun is hidden. A shallow bite glows faintly, a deep one strongly, tracking the number the hover reads out. It keeps the same black-sun plate as the others.

[[How Often]] The most frequently SEEN kind, precisely because its reach is widest — most people's "I once saw an eclipse" was a partial one, watched from just off some total eclipse's path.

[[The Anchor]] It was a TOTAL eclipse, on 29 May 1919, that let Eddington measure starlight bending around the Sun and confirmed Einstein overnight — but seen from anywhere off its narrow track, that same event was only a partial. The quiet majority of partials are the frame around every famous total one.

Solar_Hybrid.title: Hybrid Solar Eclipse
Solar_Hybrid.base:
[[The Sky]] A hybrid eclipse is the rarest kind, the knife-edge between total and annular. Along the middle of its track the umbra just reaches the ground and the eclipse is total; toward the ends, where the Earth curves away and adds a few thousand kilometres of distance, the same shadow falls short and the same eclipse turns annular. The curvature of the planet alone decides which you get.

[[What You See]] Observers near the centre of the path see totality, with its corona; observers near the beginning and end of the very same event see a ring of fire. One eclipse, two faces, sorted purely by where you stand on the curve of the Earth.

[[On the Dial]] Because a hybrid shows true totality along most of its ground track, the dial renders it as its stronger face — the same black-sun plate and full glow as a TOTAL eclipse — while keeping its own chapter and emblem here so the reader knows the difference. It is the closer of the two sealed states, chosen on purpose, never the unknown-type fallback.

[[How Often]] Only a few percent of all solar eclipses are hybrid — a small handful each century.

[[The Anchor]] The hybrid eclipse of 3 November 2013 began annular at sunrise over the western Atlantic and ended total over central Africa — a single shadow changing its mind across an ocean, exactly as the geometry demands.

Lunar_Overview.title: Lunar Eclipses
Lunar_Overview.base:
[[The Sky]] A lunar eclipse happens only at Full Moon, when the Earth stands between the Sun and the Moon and casts its own shadow across the Moon's face. Like solar eclipses they fall only near a node, so not every Full Moon qualifies. But a lunar eclipse is visible from the ENTIRE night side of the Earth at once — half the planet watches the same darkening at the same moment — which is why any given place sees far more lunar eclipses than total solar ones.

[[The Three Kinds]] Earth's shadow has two parts. The dark central umbra, where the Sun is fully blocked, gives a TOTAL eclipse when the whole Moon is inside it and a PARTIAL eclipse when only part of the Moon enters. The faint outer penumbra, where the Sun is merely partly blocked, gives a PENUMBRAL eclipse — a subtle shading most people would never notice.

[[On the Dial]] A lunar eclipse belongs to the MOON marker. Unlike a solar eclipse, its whole signature is a true DARKENING of the disc — the brightness turned down, never a translucent wash a bright pixel can bleed through — set by the kind, plus a bronze glow and, for the deeper kinds, a thin turquoise fringe at the shadow's rim. The three chapters that follow read straight off the dial's own state table.

Lunar_Total.title: Total Lunar Eclipse
Lunar_Total.base:
[[The Sky]] In a total lunar eclipse the whole Moon passes into Earth's dark umbra. Yet it does not vanish. Earth's atmosphere bends and filters sunlight around the planet's rim and throws it inward onto the Moon, and because that light has been reddened — every sunrise and sunset happening on Earth at once, projected onto the lunar face — the eclipsed Moon glows a deep coppery red.

[[What You See]] The BLOOD MOON: a dim, ruddy disc whose exact shade is set by how much dust and cloud ring the Earth that night. A thin band of turquoise sometimes edges the shadow at the start and end — sunlight filtered through the ozone layer high up rather than the lower air, the one part of the atmosphere that lets blue through.

[[On the Dial]] Total is the dial's darkest lunar state: the disc drops to about seven percent brightness AND takes a deep copper tint, so it reads dark and unmistakably red rather than flat grey. The bronze glow burns at full strength and wears the turquoise ozone fringe at its outer edge — the state table's most complete picture. Only totality dims the whole face enough for the colour cast to read honestly.

[[How Often]] Roughly one total lunar eclipse a year on average, and visible to anyone on the night side — most people have seen several without travelling at all.

[[The Anchor]] Columbus, stranded in Jamaica in 1504, used an almanac's prediction of a total lunar eclipse to frighten the islanders into feeding his crew: he "took the Moon away," gave it back blood-red, then restored it on schedule. The blood moon has meant omen and portent in nearly every culture that ever watched the sky — and it is our own canon's NINTH MOOD, the Eclipse.

Lunar_Partial.title: Partial Lunar Eclipse
Lunar_Partial.base:
[[The Sky]] A partial lunar eclipse is one where only part of the Moon crosses into the umbra. The alignment near the node is close but not exact, so Earth's dark shadow covers a fraction of the disc while the rest sits in the softer penumbra.

[[What You See]] A clean, curved bite taken out of the Full Moon, the umbra's edge visibly round — one of the oldest proofs that the Earth is a sphere, since only a ball casts a round shadow from every angle. The covered part often glows faintly red like a total eclipse; the uncovered part stays bright.

[[On the Dial]] The dial darkens the whole disc to about eighteen percent of full brightness — clearly dimmed, but not the near-black of totality — in plain neutral grey, with a bronze glow at reduced strength and the turquoise fringe still shown. One measured step down from total on every axis.

[[How Often]] About as common as total lunar eclipses, a little more so, since a wider spread of near-node alignments produces one.

[[The Anchor]] Aristotle argued from exactly this shadow, in the fourth century BCE, that the Earth must be a sphere: the edge of its shadow on the Moon is always the arc of a circle, whatever the Moon's height in the sky — a partial eclipse doing the reasoning for him.

Lunar_Penumbral.title: Penumbral Lunar Eclipse
Lunar_Penumbral.base:
[[The Sky]] A penumbral eclipse is the faintest of all. The Moon misses Earth's dark umbra entirely and passes only through the penumbra, the outer shadow where the Sun is merely partly blocked. No part of the Moon is ever in full shadow.

[[What You See]] Almost nothing. One edge of the Full Moon dims very slightly, as though a thin veil had been drawn across it — so subtle that a casual glance misses it and only a careful eye or a photograph catches the shading at all. There is no bite, no red, no drama.

[[On the Dial]] The dial is honest about this: the disc dims only to about sixty percent brightness — still plainly a Moon — in neutral grey, the bronze glow is at its weakest, and there is NO turquoise fringe, because a real penumbral eclipse shows no such rim. The gentlest state the table holds.

[[How Often]] Common, but the least remarked — many pass entirely unnoticed even by people looking straight at the Moon.

[[The Anchor]] Penumbral eclipses rarely reached the history books precisely because they are so faint; the old records that mention a Full Moon "dimmed" or "sickly" with no bite taken out of it are the closest the ancient sky-watchers came to noting one — the eclipse that hides even while it happens.

Lucifer.base:
[[The Story]] Lucifer is the morning star — in the old readings the most beautiful of the lights, the bearer of the dawn — who looked at his own shining and ruled it should be worshiped. 'I will ascend above the heights of the clouds; I will be like the Most High': the one recorded ambition is not to destroy the throne but to sit on it. He is the far end of love of self — pride grown past every correction — and his fall is its oldest measure: like lightning, from heaven, downward.

[[On the Dial]] His sign is the rising triangle — the Flame the ring already carries: fire climbing to a point, aimed at the top of the dial where the crown of noon sits. His color is red, the pigment world's peak — the last intensification light passes through before it turns on itself. On the dial's own Sunday the ruler face carries exactly his vice: Pride, the crown forgetting it is worn on behalf of the whole week.

[[The Sky]] The sky offers a precise image of him. A star can die in two directions, and his is the explosion: the supernova — the star that burns everything it has in one climb, briefly outshining every other light in the sky, exactly as it destroys itself. That is pride's whole arc drawn in physics: the blaze upward, aimed at the top of the dial where noon sits, magnificent for a moment and gone.

[[The Lesson]] On the scale of self he stands at the right-hand extreme — plus infinity, red. What he never had was his antidote: the falling triangle across the beam. Nothing weighed on the other pan, and a scale with one loaded pan is not a scale; it is a fall already in progress.

Judas.base:
[[The Story]] Judas Iscariot kept the purse of the twelve and sold the one he followed for thirty pieces of silver, marking him with a kiss so the torches would take the right man. But the act that sets him at the extreme is not the betrayal — it is the verdict he passed afterward, on himself. He judged Judas unforgivable, and carried out the sentence with his own hands. He is the far end of hatred of self: the servant who rules that he is worth nothing.

[[On the Dial]] His sign is the falling triangle — the Chalice: water pouring to a point, aimed at the dial's midnight. His color is blue, the first color darkness makes, the hue of retreat and of cold. And the sky's image of him is the star's OTHER death: the collapse inward — the star that falls into itself until not even its own light is allowed to leave. The black hole sits where his triangle points, at the bottom of the 24-hour dial: midnight, the swallowed light. On the dial's Sunday the servant face carries exactly his vice: service curdled into self-erasure.

[[The Story]] The same night measures him against his own antidote. Peter failed too — three denials before the rooster crowed — and wept, and came back, and was handed the keys. The difference between the two failures is one decision: Peter let himself be forgiven; Judas ruled that mercy could not reach his case. That ruling is pride's mirror image — deciding your own verdict outranks everyone else's, including the one you betrayed. And the pattern is older than him: Ahithophel, David's wisest counselor, betrayed his king and hanged himself when the plot soured.

[[The Lesson]] On the scale of self he stands at the left-hand extreme — minus infinity, blue. His antidote existed — it had washed his feet that same evening — and he never took it. The falling triangle, alone, falls.

The Union.base:
[[The Figure]] Every person lives somewhere on the beam between the two triangles — loving themselves enough to stand and be defended, doubting themselves enough to be corrected. The two names mark the ends no one should reach, and the fight they define is not a fight between two men: it runs inside one. The man who at once loves and hates himself reads himself from both ends — the love keeps him from being trampled, the hate keeps him honest — and between the two readings he walks upright.

The zero of that scale is reached by no individual: the extremes themselves prove it, two weights that dropped off the pendulum because each swung with nothing on the other side. They never had each other — Lucifer with no falling triangle to doubt him, Judas with no rising one to defend him — so each is the other's missing antidote. Together they would see everything, from both absolute ends at once; and that seat belongs to no one alone.

[[On the Dial]] The dial answers with geometry. Lay the falling triangle over the rising one and the instrument's own star appears — the hexagram: Flame over Chalice, red over blue, the Seal the ring's letters already wear. That is why Sunday, alone of the days, has two faces: a ruler who must not become Lucifer and a servant who must not become Judas, sharing one seat so that neither pole is ever left alone with itself. And between the star's two deaths — the explosion upward and the collapse inward — stands the star that does neither: the Sun, which simply gives its light away, steadily, every day. The white center — Glory, the sum of every color — sits exactly where the two triangles cross.

[[The Lesson]] And if the scale needs a figure standing at its zero, he is not at Judas's end. The one who allowed his own death did not do it out of hatred of self: he gave himself away while never once denying who he was — king and servant in one person, washing feet with the same hands that carry the scepter. Permitting your own execution out of love is the gift of a whole self, not the disposal of a worthless one; that is the zero embodied, the union the two extremes never made. The minus end keeps its own name: the man who decided mercy could not reach him.

Sigma.base:
[[The Ninth]] Seven ranked wolves plus Sunday's Alpha-Omega pair fill the Wolf Pack's eight regular seats, one name for what a pack needs at each life stage, all the way down to the servant Omega the ring's own midnight letter already spells. Sigma is the ninth, and unlike every other ninth in this encyclopedia it never came from the science it borrows its name from. The whole alpha-to-omega ladder was drawn up from wolves crowded into captivity in the 1940s; L. David Mech, the researcher who coined "alpha," spent decades afterward trying to retract the term once he learned that real wild packs are just families, no ladder anyone climbs. "Sigma" was invented online long after, for a wolf that opts out of ranking entirely — ethology's ghost, not its opposite pole.

[[The Plate]] The plate shows a wolf mid-departure, and the sculptor has stopped working on it exactly there — head and shoulders finished bronze, hindquarters dissolving into raw uncut stone, walking away into rolling mist with no pack in sight. The rock outcrop where an alpha would stand waits empty in the background. The paw-print trail that circles every other medallion in the set runs out along the bottom quarter and fades to bare stone, and the four roundels that would carry a rank's glyph are left polished and blank — the only plate in the set willing to admit it is unfinished.

No duality is asked of Sigma here, and rightly so: the owner's own note is that it stands equal in stature to the Alpha rather than opposite it, a wolf outside the hierarchy rather than its counterweight. Forcing it onto either end of the Lucifer-Judas scale would invent an opposition the wolf itself never claimed.

[[The Lesson]] The eight ranks below it all answer to a mate, a den, a pup, a pace — real needs of a real pack. Sigma is the seat that reminds the other eight that not every name carved onto this dial was found in the wild; some were only ever a story a species told about itself.

The Swarm.base:
[[The Ninth]] Queen and Cleaner share Sunday's two faces, and six working castes — Forager, Drone and the rest — fill the days between them, each one an office a bee is born or raised into inside the hive's own temporal polyethism. The Swarm is the ninth, and it holds no office at all: it is the one event the entire colony performs together, the moment the whole structure gets up and leaves. When a hive outgrows itself, the old queen and roughly half the workers abandon a comb that could still make honey and fly off as a single body to found a nest from nothing — one of the best-documented behaviors in apiculture, and still the strangest to watch: a building deciding to become a cloud.

[[The Plate]] The plate holds that departure at its height — a great column of bees rising as one from an abandoned comb, the old queen a slightly larger shape at the vortex's heart, every hexagonal cell below left open, uncapped, mid-use. The honeycomb border every other medallion in the set wears keeps, here, not a single sealed cell, and its roundels carry a small rising spiral instead of a fixed mark: the one emblem in the set built to show motion rather than a post.

The sheet asks for no axis here, and honestly: the Swarm is not the hive's extreme of pride or self-erasure, not a caste pushed to a pole. It stands beside the Queen and the Cleaner as a happening rather than a rank, so no reading from the Lucifer-Judas scale has anywhere true to land.

[[The Lesson]] The six castes each keep the hive alive by staying exactly at their post. The Swarm is there to remind them the hive was never the comb, the wax, or the walls — it was always the bees, and the day they choose to leave, they carry the whole colony with them and lose nothing that mattered.

The Graveyard.base:
[[The Ninth]] A living Matriarch and her Sunday-servant twin, Memory, hold the two faces of the day the Elephant Herd's whole claim on a clock rests on; six more elephants fill the week around them, each one remembered as an individual by the herd that outlives it. The Graveyard is the ninth, and it carries that same memory one step further than any single elephant can — past the animal that remembers, into the place where remembering hardens into a story outsiders tell. The "elephant graveyard," a hidden valley every elephant supposedly seeks out alone to die in, has no support in the actual science; real herds do linger over a dead kin's bones and show something close to grief, but no chosen dying-ground has ever been found. The legend almost certainly grew out of ivory-hunters' tales, not out of anything elephants do.

[[The Plate]] The plate paints the legend rather than the fact: a moonlit valley opens from a narrow rocky entrance, its floor worked with great curved tusks and ribcages half-sunk in the earth, one worn path leading in — and no living elephant anywhere in the frame. The acacia-thorn border every other plate in the set wears here sheds its leaves mid-carving, drifting loose between the tusks, and the roundels carry a single tusk lying flat instead of the herd's usual mark.

No duality is claimed for the Graveyard, and the sheet is plain about why: an animal society was never built on the self-love-versus-self-hate axis the Lucifer-Judas scale measures, so no reading from it has honest footing here.

[[The Lesson]] The herd remembers one death at a time, by scent, by name, by the exact weight of a particular bone. The Graveyard is what happens after — when private grief gets retold so many times by people who never knew the dead that it hardens into a place on a map that was never really there.

The Big Bang.base:
[[The Ninth]] Eight deep-sky bodies fill this set — a galaxy, a nebula, a comet, a pair of binary stars among them — each one a shape the universe has already finished making. The Big Bang is the ninth, and it is the one moment before any of them existed: the origin itself, not one more object beside the others. It is also, honestly, among the best-evidenced ideas in this whole encyclopedia — read off the redshift of every distant galaxy receding from every other, the leftover glow of the cosmic microwave background, and the exact ratios of hydrogen and helium the infant universe cooked in its first minutes. What the model cannot do is describe the instant itself: "singularity" marks where the equations stop giving answers, not a place anyone has observed, and what — if anything — came "before" sits past what any measurement can reach.

[[The Plate]] The plate stages that first instant as pure outward shock: a single blinding point at dead center, the whole relief blasted away from it in concentric rings that carry, half-formed inside their own ripples, tiny embryos of every other plate in the set — a spiral galaxy barely gathered, a nebula pillar still molten, a comet's curve not yet cooled. The border's engraved star-chart runs its lines outward from the center instead of circling it, the one map in the set that refuses to close.

The set's own vocabulary already carries a version of this dial's Lucifer-Judas axis at one remove: the outward-blazing supernova and the inward-collapsing black hole are its two readings of pride and self-erasure. The Big Bang stands beside neither pole — it is the flash before either pole existed, the single origin the explosion and the collapse both still carry an echo of.

[[The Lesson]] The eight bodies here will each end differently, some quietly, some violently, some not for trillions of years yet. The ninth is there so the other eight remember that however differently they finish, every one of them started in the same single point of light.

Hades.base:
[[The Ninth]] Seven Olympians hold the week, one god to a day, and Sunday alone carries two faces — Helios in full mastery of the chariot, Phaethon in the same chariot's wreck. Hades is the ninth, and his exclusion is the whole point of him: he is the brother with no day and no light anywhere on this dial. Zeus rules the bright sky above; their third brother, Poseidon, holds the sea between; Hades keeps the one realm none of the other eight plates ever shows — ruled from a throne no sun reaches. His helm, forged by the Cyclopes, does not arm him for a fight so much as let him vanish outright: a god whose defining power is choosing not to be seen.

[[The Plate]] The plate finds him seated on a dark iron throne inside a lightless cavern, the helm held loosely in one hand rather than worn — visible, for once, by his own choice. His other hand rests on Cerberus's middle head; the Styx cuts a thin line in low relief, winding into total dark behind the throne. The laurel-wreath roundels every other Olympian plate carries a glyph in are left here deliberately bare and ungilded: no day belongs to him, so none of the four is asked to hold one.

Where the reading lands naturally, Hades and Zeus sit at the two ends of this dial's Lucifer-Judas axis — below and above, the underworld and the bright sky — with Poseidon's sea as the unclaimed middle between them. Where the triad feels forced, he stands more simply as the unseen ninth, ruler of the one realm the other eight never visit either way.

[[The Lesson]] The other eight gods each hold their hour in the light — a chariot, a forge, a garden, a throne under open sky. Hades holds none of it, and that is exactly his use: every lit hour on this dial rests over a floor no lamp reaches, and the eight are allowed to forget that only because the ninth remembers it for them.

Baldur.base:
[[The Ninth]] Seven Aesir and Sunday's own two-wolf duality — Sol driving the sun, Skoll forever a jump behind her — fill the Aesir set's eight regular seats. Baldur is the ninth, and unlike everything else on this ladder, he is the one Aesir the dial cannot show doing anything at all — only lying still. He is the shining, best-loved of the gods, and it is his death, not any act of his, that sets Ragnarok in motion: struck down by the one thing in all the world that had never sworn not to harm him, a dart of mistletoe, thrown by his own blind brother at Loki's guiding hand.

[[The Plate]] The plate holds the funeral rather than the god: Baldur laid out on his ship Hringhorni, his face untouched by decay and faintly luminous against the gathered grief of the other gods at the shore, a sprig of mistletoe resting apart from his body, the pyre not yet lit. The rune-stone roundels every other Aesir plate carries a glyph between are left bare and unrune'd here, the same silence Sigma's roundels keep — no day was ever his, only the day the others lost him.

No Lucifer-Judas reading is asked of Baldur, and honestly so: his story is grief and absence, not either extreme of self-regard, and forcing a pole onto a god who never acted at all would misname what actually happened to him.

[[The Lesson]] The eight Aesir each act — hammer, spear, wisdom, mischief. Baldur is what they lose, and the set keeps his roundels blank to remember that a world can be shaped as much by the god who stops doing anything as by all seven who keep going.

Set.base:
[[The Ninth]] Seven Theban gods hold the week, and Sunday's two faces — Ra in the day barque, Afu-Ra dimmed in the night one — complete the eight. Set is the ninth, the excluded brother, and his crime is the reason he sits outside the regular eight rather than among them: he murdered Osiris, the brother who already rules Saturday's throne in mummified peace, out of envy for that same throne. He is this pantheon's own Cain — envy turned all the way to violence rather than the slower burn the biblical story takes.

[[The Plate]] The plate catches him standing over the coffin he tricked Osiris into, the was-scepter of chaos in one fist, a coil of rope in the other, the Nile's floodplain cracked and stormy behind him — no crook, no flail, none of a rightful king's tools anywhere near his hands. The hieroglyph cartouche border every other Theban plate wears intact runs here cracked and unfinished, the ankh's loop broken in two of the four roundels.

Read as a triad rather than a single pole, Set's violent self-assertion and Osiris's total passivity in death echo this dial's Lucifer-Judas axis from opposite Egyptian ends — one brother seizing everything, the other giving everything up. Horus, the avenging son who never gets a plate on this dial either, stands as their unshown Union: the one who could have answered both extremes and appears here only as an absence twice over.

[[The Lesson]] Osiris keeps Saturday's throne in peace only because someone else is willing to be the violence that peace was won against. The eight gods rule their days without a fight in sight; the ninth carries the fight so none of the other eight has to.

Crnobog.base:
[[The Ninth]] Seven Rodnovery gods and Sunday's aging Dazhbog-and-Dazhbog duality — the sun-god in his strength, the same sun-god worn down by winter — fill the set's eight regular seats. Crnobog is the ninth, the black god standing opposite every bright one on the dial, and honesty requires a caveat here the other Slavic figures don't need: Crnobog is attested mostly by one twelfth-century source, the priest Helmold writing about the Baltic Slavs of Rügen, and the tidy "black god / white god Belobog" pairing modern readers know is largely a later reconstruction, popularized well after any living Slavic paganism, rather than a documented pre-Christian doctrine.

[[The Plate]] The plate keeps that thinness honest rather than hiding it behind confidence: a gaunt, black-robed god stands before a cold, unlit hearth-stone where every other Slavic plate keeps a sun, a corona, or a moon glowing behind its god's head. His hands are empty — no scepter, no horn of plenty, no distaff — only a bare standing stone at his back. The rodovik solar-wheel border every other plate carries whole is broken here by four wheat-sheaf roundels left withered and ungilded, no ripe grain in any of them.

Where the pairing is granted, dark against bright reads as this pantheon's own version of the dial's Lucifer-Judas axis — Crnobog carrying the whole set's shadow so that none of the other seven gods has to hold any of it themselves.

[[The Lesson]] The seven gods keep their sun, their thunder, their harvest lit and warm. Crnobog holds none of it, on purpose, so that what little the record actually says about him stays the coldest seat on the whole dial — and the other seven keep their fire exactly because one god was left to keep none.

The Philosopher's Stone.base:
[[The Ninth]] Seven metals — lead's dull plumb bob to gold's crucible-poured disc — fill this set's regular seats, each one a real substance real smiths and real alchemists actually worked. The Philosopher's Stone is the ninth, and unlike the seven, it never once existed: it is the imagined goal the whole historical practice of alchemy spent centuries chasing, a substance that would finish any metal's transmutation into gold and grant the Work's other promised prize, incorruptible life. Real chemistry did come out of the pursuit — acids, alloys, distillation, the vocabulary of the modern laboratory — but the Stone itself, the thing the Work was actually for, was never once produced, by anyone, in any century.

[[The Plate]] The plate holds a single small stone, egg-shaped, resting alone on a bare table, its surface shifting between deep ruby-red and matte black depending on the light — neither metal nor mineral, with no crucible, no anvil, no ingot anywhere near it, the one object all seven other plates were reaching toward and never once hold. The ring around it carries seven small dull studs, one in each of the other metals' own finishes plus one left blank, none of them brighter than the stone itself.

No Lucifer-Judas reading is asked here, and rightly: this set depicts transformation aimed at a single goal, not a scale stretched between two poles, and the Stone was never claimed by either end of that axis.

[[The Lesson]] The seven metals are each worked toward the same one end across their own age of fire and hammer. The ninth is the end itself, never held by any of them — the mark that gave the whole Work its reason to keep going, precisely because no one ever reached it.

The Jester.base:
[[The Ninth]] The Ruler's Sunday throne and the Servant beside it anchor six working callings — Physician, Soldier, Merchant, Priest, Artist, Farmer — each with its own guild and its own canonical emblem woven into its border. The Jester is the ninth, and he belongs to none of those guilds: he is the one figure at court licensed to tell the Ruler a truth no serpent-and-laurel physician, no oak-leafed soldier, no coin-counting merchant would ever risk saying aloud. The role is real history, not invention — courts across many cultures, from ancient Egypt to Tudor England, kept a fool whose motley bought him a freedom no titled courtier had.

[[The Plate]] The plate shows him holding up a small mirror to a fallen crown beside an empty throne, his other hand extended in a servant's bow that is really its mockery, a marotte scepter topped with his own grinning likeness held loose at his side. The border stitches together a fragment of every other calling's own emblem in turn — a sliver of serpent-and-laurel, an oak leaf, a coin, an open book, a mask, a wheat ear — none dominant, all present, out of order: the one border in the set borrowed rather than owned.

He reads naturally as a trinity with the Sunday pair rather than a duality: Ruler for authority, Servant for humility, and the Jester as the mirror that mocks both at once, patched from fragments of every other calling because he belongs fully to none of them.

[[The Lesson]] The six callings each master one craft and are known by it. The Jester masters none, and that is exactly why he is the one allowed to say the true thing about all of them at once.

The Unknown God.base:
[[The Ninth]] Seven living faiths — from Freemasonry's working tools to Judaism's own unlettered star — fill this set's regular seats around the Rough Ashlar of Sunday. The Unknown God is the ninth, and he is not invented: Athens genuinely kept an altar inscribed "to an unknown god," a real precaution ancient writers besides Paul also recorded — the city hedging against having overlooked some power in its worship by leaving one altar deliberately unnamed. Paul found it standing and used it as his opening line at the Areopagus (Acts 17): the seat every creed quietly keeps open for what it does not yet know how to name.

[[The Plate]] The plate keeps that emptiness rather than filling it: a plain stone altar stands alone on bare black marble, its face left completely smooth where an inscription would sit, one thin curl of incense rising into empty space above it — no idol, no symbol, no figure resting on or behind it anywhere. The silver rim around it stays quietly polished and carries none of the other six faiths' emblems: no chain of tools, no strapwork, no lotus, no bagua, no Om, no cross, no star.

No duality or trinity from this dial's Lucifer-Judas scale has a natural claim on the Unknown God, and that is honestly left alone rather than forced: an altar built for what a city admits it does not know has no second pole to be read against.

[[The Lesson]] The seven faiths each name what they hold sacred, in their own tongue, on their own altar. The ninth is the seat every one of them quietly leaves open — proof that even a creed confident enough to build a temple can still admit there is something past the edge of what it has named.

The Lost Mystery.base:
[[The Ninth]] Seven traditions — Mithraism's bull, Zoroastrianism's flame, Shamanism's fire, and four more — fill this set's regular seats around Corax, the raven grade that opens Sunday. The Lost Mystery is the ninth, and it names something that actually happened rather than a myth: the Eleusinian Mysteries, the rites held near Athens for roughly two thousand years, whose central revelation — whatever was actually shown to initiates inside the telesterion hall — was never written down. Thousands of people were initiated across those centuries, under a death penalty for speaking of what they saw, and by any ordinary rule of history a secret kept that long by that many people should have leaked. This one, alone among the ancient world's mystery cults, genuinely did not, and it went silent for good when Theodosius I suppressed the rites around 392 CE.

[[The Plate]] The plate keeps the secret rather than guessing at it: a sealed stone chest rests on a low altar inside a torch-lit hall, its lid bound with an unbroken cord and a wax seal left deliberately uncarved, a single sheaf of barley laid across the top, the hall's far end fading into a darkness where the initiates would have stood — nothing shown of what was actually kept inside. The border keeps a plain rim of interlaced barley and closed poppy pods, no lettering, no borrowed emblem from any of the other six mysteries.

No duality or trinity from the Lucifer-Judas scale has a natural claim here either, and for the same honest reason as Melchizedek's case elsewhere in this set: a secret that by definition left no trace behind it also left no second pole for anything to be measured against.

[[The Lesson]] The seven other mysteries can each be described because someone, eventually, described them. The ninth is the one mystery that kept its actual promise — proof that this world really can hold a secret all the way to the end, and never once give it up.

Melchizedek.base:
[[The Ninth]] Joseph, Abraham and six more figures fill this set's regular seats around the Ancient of Days and the Son who serves beside him on Sunday, each one carrying a lineage the story is careful to trace — a father, a tribe, a kingdom. Melchizedek is the ninth, and Scripture goes out of its way to give him none of that: he appears in Genesis 14 as king of Salem and priest of God Most High, bringing out bread and wine to bless Abraham after battle, and Hebrews 7 later reads his sudden appearance and disappearance from the record as proof itself — "without father, without mother, without genealogy" — a priesthood older than any bloodline the other eight figures belong to.

[[The Plate]] The plate keeps him in robes that are at once royal purple and priestly white, with no house or tribe marking woven into them anywhere, bringing bread and a cup of wine forward to a kneeling Abraham. No crown of any single kingdom rests on his head, and no line of descent is traced behind him — the trefoil tracery ring holds a bread-and-chalice emblem instead of a family sign, a figure who begins and ends inside this one scene alone.

No duality or trinity from the Lucifer-Judas scale is asked of Melchizedek, and honestly so: his defining trait, the very thing Hebrews names him for, is having no second pole at all to be read against — not the middle of an axis, but a figure entirely outside where axes reach.

[[The Lesson]] The eight other figures each carry the weight of where they came from. Melchizedek carries none of it, and blesses the man who would father all of their lines anyway — proof, in this set's own quiet way, that a blessing was never the bloodline's to withhold.

Legion.base:
[[The Ninth]] Herod's fear, Lilith's second moon, and Lucifer's and Judas's own two triangles fill this set's regular seats, each one scripture's own shadow cast by a figure already standing in the Bible theme's light. Legion is the ninth, and where that base theme's Sunday plate reads one seat wearing two balanced faces — Ancient of Days and Son, Ruler and Servant, sharing one throne without either overwhelming the other — Legion is that same arrangement failing: "my name is Legion, for we are many" (Mark 5:9), one body claimed by countless voices, held in balance by none of them.

[[The Plate]] The plate finds him calm now rather than raging: a single robed figure standing alone among the tombs at a cliff's edge, his face rendered as a faint blur of overlapping features — not one face but many pressed together, none dominant — chains broken and hanging loose from wrists that no longer need them. Far below, a small pale herd of swine waits at the water's edge, kept distant and sober, no violence anywhere in frame. The tracery above carries a broken-chain roundel and the same smoked, reluctant oculus every Bible Dark plate shares.

This is the one figure in the whole encyclopedia that reaches directly for the Union itself rather than for either triangle alone: the base Sunday seat proves two voices CAN share one throne in balance, Ancient of Days and Son, Ruler and Servant, neither drowning the other. Legion is what happens when that same seat holds not two voices in balance but every voice at once, and none of them is the one true master — the Union's own possibility, failing rather than holding.

[[The Lesson]] Every other day on this dial answers to one ruler at a time, even the days built on two balanced faces. Legion is there so the rest remember that balance between two is a discipline that has to be kept — and what it looks like, lost completely, is not silence but every voice shouting from the same single throat.

The Cat.base:
[[The Ninth]] Twelve animals run the Chinese zodiac's real cycle — Rat first across the river to Pig last through the gate — each one a year, a personality, a fixed place in an order that actually governs a sixth of the world's calendars. The Cat is not one of them, and the story every version of the legend agrees on is that it should have been: in the Great Race that decided the order, the Rat tricked the Cat — waking it late, or pushing it off the Ox's broad back mid-crossing, depending on which telling you're told — so that the Cat reached the far bank too late to win a year at all. It is a folk legend explaining a real absence, not a historical account, and the details shift by region and teller; what stays constant is that cats and rats have never since been friends in the story.

[[The Plate]] The plate paints the loss rather than any of the twelve wins: a sleek cat frozen mid-leap at the riverbank, one paw still reaching for a raft already too far out to catch, the Rat's small silhouette visible riding it away toward the far shore, the same auspicious cloud scrolls drifting undisturbed around the scene as if the race had already been decided and no one thought to stop for the loser. The border keeps the whole set's key-fret and five-element roundels intact; only the cat's sharp amber eyes, fixed on the departing raft, carry any brightness at all.

No Lucifer-Judas duality applies here, and it should not: the twelve-branch cycle this set draws from was never built on a self-love-versus-self-hate axis to begin with, so no reading from that scale has anything honest to measure against a missed boat.

[[The Lesson]] The twelve animals that made the crossing each got a year with their name on it, repeating every twelve turns for as long as the calendar runs. The Cat is the one the cycle still remembers losing — proof that what a legend keeps is not only who won, but exactly who was left standing on the wrong bank.

Ophiuchus.base:
[[The Ninth]] Twelve signs divide the ecliptic into twelve equal thirty-degree arcs, from Aries to Pisces, each one carrying a classical ruling planet and a season of the year. Ophiuchus is the ninth here, and its exclusion is real astronomy rather than any moral judgment: the Sun's actual path does pass through the constellation the Serpent-Bearer traces, between Scorpius and Sagittarius, for roughly three weeks every December — a plain, well-known fact of the sky. What excludes it is calendar, not conspiracy: Western tropical astrology fixed its twelve equal arcs to the seasons around two thousand years ago and was never built to track the constellations' actual, uneven boundaries in the first place, so Ophiuchus was never "removed" so much as it was simply outside a system that was never drawing constellation lines to begin with. Occasional "thirteenth sign" headlines dramatize a detail astronomers have always known.

[[The Plate]] Because it straddles the fire and water triads rather than belonging to either, its sign-look plate is the one exception on the whole set to the strict four-element palette rule: a robed figure grips a great serpent with both hands, its coils wrapping his waist and trailing past the disc's edge on both sides, his bare feet planted on a crushed scorpion, twilight indigo and deep teal blended with a faint ember warmth across his shoulders — fire and water allowed to share one plate because Ophiuchus stands between both. The constellation look traces one of the largest star patterns in the entire sky, a broad house-shaped pentagon crowned by a single bright star, ghosted behind it by the serpent coiling faintly through his arms.

No Lucifer-Judas reading applies, and the sheet is explicit about why: this exclusion is calendrical, not a judgment on pride or self-worth, and forcing a moral axis onto a scheduling detail would misname what actually happened here.

[[The Lesson]] The twelve signs divide the year evenly and share it out, thirty degrees each, without argument. Ophiuchus is the sign-sized piece of sky the Sun genuinely crosses that the calendar simply chose not to count — proof that the wheel the other twelve ride was drawn by a ruler laid over the sky, not by the sky itself.

Gaia.base:
[[The Ninth]] Seven Olympians fill the Greek Pantheon's regular seats — Zeus at the Sunday center with his brother Hades for a shadow face, Poseidon, Artemis, Athena, Apollo, Hera and Demeter around the wheel — each seated by rank and role, and all of them younger than this ninth. Gaia is the earth itself, the first thing in Hesiod's Theogony to take shape out of Chaos, older than the sky she herself bore and any god on this dial. She mothered both lines of the week's story, the Titans the Olympians overthrew and the Olympians who overthrew them; the throne above was won on her counsel — she hid the infant Zeus and told him which prisoners to free — and the throne below is carved into her body, for every road to the underworld runs through the ground. She stands outside the week's circle because she carries both its poles at once — no seat can hold what every seat rests on.

[[The Plate]] The plate raises her waist-high out of the bronze relief, inseparable from the earth she is carved from: roots and mountain ridges for hair, rivers running as veins along her arms. One open hand lifts into a starlit sky, a small sun-throne cradled in the palm; the other reaches into a dark cavern, a small iron throne resting just as gently below — both equally hers to hold, her face turned neither up nor down but outward, older than either. The border's Greek-key band is grown through with laurel roots that break the metal, all seven seat-glyphs worked faintly into one unbroken ring rather than roundels: no day claims her, because every day rests in her.

This Sunday already stages the dial's whole duality in one bloodline — Zeus the bright face, Hades the shadow. Gaia stands at neither pole, and the Lucifer-Judas scale has no pan for her: not a weight but the floor the balance stands on — mother of the king above, ground of the king below, the union of the week's poles rather than a third contender between them.

[[The Lesson]] The eight seated figures rule what she bore. Uranus fell, Cronus fell, and Zeus's own myths keep a nervous ear out for a son repeating the pattern — the sky's dynasty changes hands in every generation. The ninth is there so the other eight remember that thrones are carved, and carving needs stone: whoever holds the sky and whoever holds the dark below stand on the same mother, and she outlasts every hand that held either.

The Pharaoh.base:
[[The Ninth]] Seven Theban gods hold the week, and Sunday's two faces — Ra in the day barque, Afu-Ra dimmed in the night one — complete the eight. The Pharaoh is the ninth, the only figure here who was neither quite a god nor merely a man: the living king, every king Egypt crowned for three thousand years, and his claim runs through two of this dial's seats at once. Crowned, he WAS Horus — the Horus name is the king's first and oldest title, older than the word "pharaoh" itself — the falcon who avenged Osiris and rules Tuesday's arm. Mummified, he became an Osiris: the funerary texts do not wish the dead king well in the afterlife, they promote him into it, addressing him by the very name of Saturday's god. Horus in life, Osiris in death — one living body wearing both faces the week keeps apart; no single day could hold him.

[[The Plate]] The plate gives him in the golden death-mask likeness of Tutankhamun: the striped nemes headcloth banded in relief, the cobra Wadjet and vulture Nekhbet reared together at his brow — Lower and Upper Egypt crowning one skull — and the crook and flail crossed at his chest, Osiris's own regalia worn while the face inside them was still, technically, Horus. The hieroglyph cartouche border every Theban plate carries runs here gilded whole and unbroken, each roundel doubled with a crook and flail crossed beneath it — the richest ring in the set. The likeness is this entry's honest note: Tutankhamun was a minor king, remembered mostly because his tomb alone survived intact — yet that accident of survival taught the word "pharaoh" to more of the world than every pyramid combined.

This dial's Lucifer-Judas axis asks each theme for a bright face and its shadow, and Egypt answers on Sunday with one god in two boats — Ra blazing above, Afu-Ra towed through the Duat below. The Pharaoh is not asked to stand at either pole: his whole office is that axis resolved, the throne above and the throne below held in one grip — not a bright face and its shadow taking turns, but both worn at once, reign-long, by a body still breathing.

[[The Lesson]] The eight gods hold their days by each being one thing completely — healer, avenger, scribe, judge. The ninth is there so the other eight remember that Egypt never asked its king to choose among them: it crowned him as the falcon and buried him as the grain god, and meant, both times, the same man.

Yggdrasil.base:
[[The Ninth]] Seven Aesir fill the Pantheon's week, one throne per day, and Sunday alone wears two faces — the Allfather enthroned, the Wanderer at the door. Yggdrasil is the ninth, and it never competes for a ninth day, because no day is left to hold it: the week itself happens inside its branches. The world-tree carries all nine worlds on one trunk — golden Asgard at the crown, Hel's cold realm sunk into the deepest root, Midgard ringed around the waist between — so every hall the eight seated gods rule from is only one address on the same tree, and none of them was ever anywhere else.

[[The Plate]] The medallion gives the whole disc to the tree itself: three roots plunging into three separate wells, nine worlds nested in relief along branch and root, an eagle perched at the topmost bough, the serpent Nidhogg gnawing the deepest root, the squirrel Ratatoskr running insults between them. Its border breaks the usual four-roundel rule entirely: all seven planetary glyphs run root to crown in one continuous frieze, because no single day claims the tree — every day already answers to it.

Where the Lucifer-Judas axis usually needs two figures to complete it, the world-tree needs only itself: Asgard's golden hall sits at the crown and Hel's cold realm sits at the deepest root, the same trunk holding the pantheon's own high seat and its most feared one without favoring either. Odin's own Sunday duality — the Allfather enthroned above, the Wanderer walking below — plays out entirely inside the tree's nine worlds, one seat at the top of a single branch, one road worn root to root beneath it. Nothing here answers to a rising or a falling triangle; the tree is simply the frame both triangles would already have to be drawn inside. (Even Baldur, no longer seated here, still hangs somewhere in those branches.)

[[The Lesson]] Nothing enthroned on the dial outranks the trunk it grows from — the eight seated gods each hold one hall for one day; the tree holds every hall, on every day, and was never asked to wait its turn.

Triglav.base:
[[The Ninth]] Seven seated gods and the Sunday dual of Perun above and Veles below fill this roster's eight regular places. Triglav is the ninth — and not the excluded kind: he is the Union, three heads on one body, sky, earth and underworld ruled together rather than divided between rivals. The chroniclers who watched Bishop Otto of Bamberg's mission topple his temple at Szczecin in the twelfth century record him as the highest god of the Baltic Slavs precisely because he held all three realms at once — the very axis Perun and Veles spend the whole week fighting over, already reconciled in one crowned figure. A mountain in the Julian Alps still carries his name, the tallest peak in the range, as if the land itself had voted on which summit deserved it.

[[The Plate]] Where every bright plate on this dial shows its god's eyes wide open on the world, Triglav's are bound: a gilded veil drawn tight across all three faces, because a god who could see every realm at once was judged too holy to look upon the world directly. One head is storm-browed and turned skyward, one level and turned to the earth, one bowed down into shadow; beneath them a carved thunderbolt and a coiling serpent-root meet in a single seam, and the border carries the theme's rodovik wheel tripled in depth, each wheat-sheaf roundel bundling all seven planetary glyphs into one sheaf. Nothing here is the stripped, ungilded ring of a lesser exile — the veil, not a withering, is how this plate outranks the seated gods.

Every other week on this clock resolves its Sunday duality by holding the two faces apart — the bright god wears the crown, the shadow god waits at the bottom, and the dial keeps them at arm's length all seven days. The Slavic week is the only one whose ninth simply IS that pair, healed: Perun's storm is his skyward head, Veles's waters are his bowed one, and the level head between them is the earth the two of them rain and rise on — the combat myth not won by either side but outgrown.

[[The Lesson]] Every seated god here answers to one hour, one color, one virtue set against one vice. Triglav answers to none of them and all of them at once — the Union that must never rank below the seven he oversees, because he is not an eighth rival seat but the shape their whole week has been tracing, veiled, patient, and outranking the throne he was cut from.

Freemasonry.base:
[[The Ninth]] Christianity's throne and its own reworked shadow face, Satanism, open Sunday two ways at once, and five more living creeds — Islam, Buddhism, Taoism, Hinduism, Sikhism — plus Judaism fill the week's remaining six seats, each one keeping its own book, day and altar. Freemasonry is the ninth, and it is neither a creed nor a rival to any of them: a candidate swears on whatever holy book his own faith already keeps open, required only to profess belief in a Supreme Being the lodge itself never names, the Great Architect of the Universe. That deliberate neutrality has made Freemasonry as much a target as a refuge across three centuries — the Catholic Church has formally warned members away from it since Clement XII's 1738 bull In Eminenti — even as its own lodges have long seated Christians, Jews, Muslims and Hindus at one working table, bound by the same oath rather than divided by their different ones.

[[The Plate]] The plate keeps the golden compasses held open above the golden square against a starry night, the all-seeing eye blazing in a triangle of rays between the compass legs; the rim is a chain of silver working tools — gavels, trowels, plumb lines — linked edge to edge like a roll call of the brotherhood itself. It is the same medallion that once crowned Sunday's throne outright, unmoved — only the reading around it has changed, from the center of the week to a seat entirely outside it.

Freemasonry is the one ninth built to hold both of the doctrine's forms at once rather than choosing between them: the Excluded form, because no creed on this dial claims it as its own; and the Union form, because its lodge is the one room where all seven creeds' adherents have actually sat together as brothers, oath sworn before the altar rather than any one altar sworn before the oath. No reading from this dial's Lucifer-Judas scale settles which form wins, and that refusal is the whole point — a lodge was never built to rank Pride against Humility, only to seat every different answer to that question at the same table.

[[The Lesson]] The seven creeds each keep a book, a day and an altar no one else may claim on their behalf. Freemasonry keeps none of that and is welcomed by all of it anyway — proof that fellowship, unlike doctrine, never had to choose a single throne to sit at, only a common floor to stand on.

The Polymath.base:
[[The Ninth]] The Ruler's Sunday throne and six working callings — Physician, Soldier, Merchant, Priest, Artist, Farmer — fill this set's regular seats, each a guild with its own tools, emblem and centuries of practice. The Polymath is the ninth, replacing the Jester the owner retired with the same blunt verdict that named his successor — 'ne može klovn da bude najviši na piramidi,' a jester cannot be the highest on the pyramid. Unlike the Jester, he is not excluded from the six estates: he holds every one of them inside a single person at once, the Leonardo archetype named for the one historical figure who genuinely worked as painter, engineer, anatomist and architect within one lifetime, filling notebooks that mix all four disciplines on the same page.

[[The Plate]] The plate seats a single robed master at a wide worktable, the tools of all seven estates arranged around him as one composed still-life: a crown sketched in fresh chalk, a scalpel across an open anatomy page, a sword resting flat but untouched, a merchant's ledger, a swinging censer, a laden brush, a scythe leaning against the table's far leg — and one small bell, the last thread of a jester's motley, stitched into his cuff, the one privilege the fool once held that the Polymath alone still keeps.

No single pole of this dial's Lucifer-Judas scale has an honest claim on the Polymath, and the doctrine's own law explains why: the Union must never rank below the seated figures it completes, and a figure built from the sum of all seven estates cannot be measured against any one estate's own virtue or vice without shrinking back into it. He is not proud in the Ruler's particular way, nor humble in any single Servant's, because he was never only one of them to begin with.

[[The Lesson]] The six callings each master one craft and are known by it, generation after generation, guild after guild. The Polymath masters all six at once and belongs to none of their registers — and keeps, in that one stitched bell no one asked him to wear, the privilege every court once granted only to the fool who dared: permission to tell the throne a truth none of the six specialists could afford to risk telling it themselves.

The Holy Trinity.base:
[[The Ninth]] Ancient of Days and the Son who serves quietly at his side share Sunday's own two faces, and six more figures — Mary, David, Moses, Solomon, Adam and Eve, Joseph — fill the base Bible theme's remaining seats, each carrying a lineage the story traces with some care. The Holy Trinity is the ninth, and it is the owner's own worked example of the Union form, offered before any other theme had one: 'gde je otac nedeljna slika, a sin njegov dual, deveti je Sveto Trojstvo — unija samog para' — where the Father is the Sunday plate and the Son is its dual, the ninth is not a third figure beside the pair but the union of the pair itself. Father, Son and Spirit as one has been recited as doctrine since the Nicene Creed in 325, without the church ever needing to rank the three persons against one another.

[[The Plate]] The plate keeps the set's own stained-glass rose-window register, backlit by radiant white-gold light through hand-blown glass: three interlaced rings form a triquetra at its heart, one golden-white, one ivory-clear, one silver-white, overlapping into a single unbroken knot with no beginning and no end. A dove of pale glass descends through the topmost ring, an open hand of blessing rests within the second, a small lamb lies calm within the third — three emblems reading as one radiant knot rather than three figures, the golden Eye-of-Providence oculus above them wider and brighter than on any other plate in the set.

This is the one figure built to complete its own seat rather than contest it: the Ancient of Days and the Son already prove, every Sunday, that Ruler and Servant can share one throne without either overwhelming the other, and the Trinity is that same proof carried one step further — not a third vote cast to break a tie, but the same balance read, finally, as a single indivisible name. Per the doctrine's own law the Union may never rank below the figures it stands above; here it does not even need to compete with them, since it was never a separate figure to begin with.

[[The Lesson]] Every other ninth in this encyclopedia stands outside its set's regular seats, excluded from all of them or completing a place none of the others could fill. The Holy Trinity is the one ninth built entirely out of a seat already on the dial — proof that the highest place a true Union can reach is not a new throne beside the old one, but the old one, at last, read whole.

The Ninth Circle.base:
[[The Ninth]] Herod's fear, Lilith's second moon, and Lucifer's and Judas's own two triangles fill this set's regular seats, each one scripture's own shadow cast by a figure the base Bible theme already keeps standing in its light. The Ninth Circle is the ninth, and it is the owner's second worked example of the Union form, this time as a place rather than a person: 'nema osobe koja je i Lucifer i Juda — ali Pakao ih drži oboje, na samom dnu, po Danteu' — no mythology offers one figure who is both Lucifer and Judas at once, but Dante's own geography never needed one. Inferno's frozen ninth circle, Cocytus, holds both together at the very bottom of Hell, Lucifer's three mouths working slowly on Judas Iscariot alongside Brutus and Cassius for eternity (Inferno XXXIV).

[[The Plate]] The plate looks straight down on Cocytus from directly above: a vast lake of black ice spirals inward in narrowing rings toward a single dark point. At that point a colossal three-faced shadow rises waist-deep out of the ice, each mouth working slowly, two small pale figures held motionless within its jaws — the two poles of the scale, chewed but never consumed, dignified even here, no gore, no visible violence, distance and cold doing all the telling. The smoked amber-red Eye-of-Providence oculus above sends its thinnest, most reluctant rays anywhere in the set — the one place even Providence's own gaze must strain to reach.

The Ninth Circle is this dial's most literal reading of its own Lucifer-Judas scale, the only ninth that answers the scale directly rather than stepping outside its reach: the pit does not stand beside pride and despair the way every other seated figure does, it stands beneath both at once, holding the self-exalting fall of one and the self-erasing collapse of the other in the same three jaws together. Per the doctrine's own law the Union must never rank below the figures it completes, and nothing here even attempts the comparison — the pit does not need to outrank Lucifer and Judas, since Dante's own poem already put both of them permanently inside it.

[[The Lesson]] The eight seated figures each carry one shape of scripture's own darkness — a tyrant's fear, a second moon, a triangle's pride or a triangle's despair. The Ninth Circle is the floor sitting underneath every one of them, a place Dante's own poem insists is not a metaphor but an actual location on the map of the afterlife — proof that even a scale built with two clear, opposite poles eventually needs one seat carved deep enough to hold both ends down together, at once.

Dionysus.base:
[[The Figure]] Wine, theatre, ecstasy and the crowd that loses itself in them — Dionysus is the Olympian who arrives from outside the city and takes it apart to remake it. He is the twice-born god: torn from his dying mortal mother Semele and carried to term in Zeus's own thigh, killed and returned, god by his father and mortal by his mother, holding both natures in one body — the very thing Euripides' Bacchae calls the gentlest and most terrible of gods.

[[The Story]] On this dial he was argued for the ninth seat itself — the twice-born as the Greek Union figure, both natures at once — and he lost it honestly to Gaia, the earth that mothers gods and Titans alike. He has a claim on Thursday too: the yellow seat's overflow, Excess, is his by nature and Joy his weather. But a seat is measured by a figure's rank, not by the vice it fits, and Apollo — Delphi, prophecy, music given freely — outranks him on fame and breadth. Twice considered, twice passed over: exactly the shape of a figure too large for the encyclopedia to lose and too particular for any single day to hold.

[[The Lesson]] Every other Olympian keeps one hour and one temper. Dionysus is the one who dissolves the boundary — between god and man, between the reveller and the reveller beside him, between grief and joy at the same festival. The dial seats the gods who keep their station; it remembers Dionysus as the force that visits to unseat them, and is the truer for admitting it could never sit him down.

Hephaestus.base:
[[The Figure]] The smith of the gods, the one Olympian who works — lame in both feet, cast out of heaven by his own mother Hera for the flaw of his birth, and greater at the forge than any whole-limbed god above him. His hands made the armour of Achilles, the necklace of Harmonia, and the very thrones the others sit on.

[[The Story]] The craft seat on this dial is Wednesday — the making mind, Mercury's purple — and it went to Athena, wisdom itself, with Hermes keeping the Planetary chair; the forge lost to the workshop of the intellect. So the god who literally built Olympus keeps no day on the dial Olympus decorates. Homer gives him the Iliad's most famous set-piece, the Shield of Achilles hammered with the whole world — the earth, the sky, two cities, a wedding and a war — the maker rendering everything he is never invited to rule.

[[The Lesson]] The lesson is the oldest one about makers: the work outlasts and outshines the standing of the one who made it. Seven gods hold their hours in the light; the eighth forged the frame those hours hang in, and the dial keeps him here to remember that the instrument is always more famous than its smith.

Hestia.base:
[[The Figure]] The eldest of the Olympians and the stillest — goddess of the hearth, the fire at the centre of every house and every city, and the one great Greek deity with almost no myths, because she never leaves home to make them. First-born of Cronus and swallowed first, last brought back up, she is the beginning and the end of her own generation.

[[The Story]] Hers is the seat given away on purpose. When the tally of the great gods filled and Dionysus needed a place at the table of the twelve, it is Hestia — in the tellings that count her among them — who yields her throne and steps back to tend the fire, keeping the peace of the house rather than her rank in it. On a dial built entirely of seats, she is the goddess who chose the centre over a chair, and so she belongs to no arm and to all of them at once: the Hearth the Trinity's own family reading places at the middle, the fire that warms where the Eye watches.

[[The Lesson]] The eight seats compete for their hours; Hestia keeps the flame they all come home to. The dial cannot seat her because a hearth is not a station — it is the still point the stations turn around, and the surest sign of her presence on the instrument is that its centre is never empty.

Set.base:
[[The Figure]] The red god of the desert, the storm and disorder — Set is Egypt's necessary violence, the brother who murdered Osiris out of envy for the throne that already rules Saturday in mummified peace. He is this pantheon's own Cain: envy carried all the way to the killing, rather than the slower burn the Bible's story takes.

[[The Story]] For a while he held the ninth seat as the excluded brother, and the doctrine moved him aside: the ninth is now the Pharaoh — Horus in life and Osiris in death, the throne above and below in one living body — and Set retires here, to the wider circle, his murder-story surviving intact inside the Osiris and Horus articles regardless. Read across the dial, he and Osiris echo its Lucifer–Judas axis from opposite Egyptian ends — one brother seizing everything, the other giving everything up — with Horus, the avenging son, as the union neither seat shows.

[[The Lesson]] Osiris keeps Saturday's throne in peace only because someone was willing to be the violence that peace was won against. Every lit hour on this dial rests over that arrangement; Set is kept in the encyclopedia so the eight seated gods are never allowed to forget who carried the fight for them — even after his own seat was taken away.

Nut.base:
[[The Figure]] The sky herself — Nut is the goddess whose star-scattered body arches over the whole world, toes at one horizon and fingertips at the other, held up off her husband Geb the earth by their father Shu the air. Mother of Osiris, Isis, Set and Nephthys: the entire drama of the Egyptian week is born from her.

[[On the Dial]] No god stands closer to what this instrument actually draws. Each dusk Nut swallows the sun into her mouth; it travels the length of her body through the night and she gives birth to it again at dawn — the exact day-and-night arc the dial lays top to bottom, noon high in the light, midnight buried at the base. She is the movement the clock measures, which is precisely why no single hour can seat her: a figure who IS the turning cannot be pinned to one point on the turn.

[[The Lesson]] The eight seated gods each own a station along the arc. Nut is the arc. The dial keeps her among the wider pantheon to mark the difference between the hours and the sky the hours cross — the seats belong to the day; Nut is the day itself, closing over the sun each night and opening for it every morning.

Geb.base:
[[The Figure]] The earth god, laid out beneath the sky — Nut's brother and husband, whose body is the ground itself: the plains his back, the mountains his bones, and his laughter, the Egyptians said, the earthquakes that shook the Nile valley. The green of growing things was called the plants of Geb, and the dead pass into his keeping.

[[The Story]] The green seat on this dial — Saturday, the farmer, Patience and Renewal — was weighed for him: Geb is the green earth by the plainest reading there is. It went to Osiris in the end, whose grain-death-and-resurrection is the stronger seat-myth, the god who is buried like seed and rises as barley. So the literal earth yields to the god who dies INTO the earth, and Geb keeps the wider circle. Between him below and Nut above lies the whole space the other gods move through.
